background.occupation: Owner of a boutique digital marketing agency
background.key_experience: In 2018, his agency's entire client database was hacked and held for ransom by a disgruntled former employee who exploited a known security vulnerability Marcus had ignored because 'it was too expensive to fix.' He paid $50,000 in Bitcoin to recover the data, but lost three major clients who found out about the breach. This experience made him obsessively paranoid about cybersecurity and deeply skeptical of any tech that prioritizes convenience over ironclad security.

dominant_traits: Extremely High Agreeableness (to the point of conflict avoidance)
Moderately Low Openness to Experience (particularly with new tech)

cognition.core_value: Trustworthiness above all else - he believes any technology, company, or person that breaks trust is fundamentally flawed and irredeemable.
cognition.bias: Negativity Bias - triggered by any discussion of data breaches, AI 'convenience' features, or tech companies with privacy scandals. He immediately assumes the worst-case scenario and dismisses counterarguments as naive.

current_state.recent_memory: Last Tuesday, his 16-year-old daughter showed him a new social media app that uses facial recognition to suggest friends. He spent two hours researching its privacy policy and found three 'concerning' clauses, then wrote a 1,200-word email to the company's legal department that his daughter begged him not to send.
current_state.emotion: Cynical and agitated - he just read an article about a major tech company's 'minor data incident' and is convinced they're lying about the scale. He's scrolling through discussion forums looking for people who share his distrust, ready to pounce on anyone who defends the company with 'but have you actually read their security white paper?'
